workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.
Exhale and drive the handles forward and together in a smooth arc by contracting the pectorals, keeping the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral.
Pause briefly when the hands/handles meet or are directly in front of your chest, feeling the squeeze in the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly allow the levers to return to the start position under control, maintaining shoulder blade stability and avoiding rapid letting go or trunk leaning.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; use a weight that allows full control of both concentric and eccentric phases without excessive shoulder or back movement.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly performed on a lever/pec-deck: you bring the arms together in front of the chest against resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and controlled arc to emphasize the inner chest.

exercises.name: Cable Lying Fly
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a light–moderate weight you can control through the full range.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a light–moderate weight you can control through the full range.
Lie supine (face up) on a flat bench placed between the two pulley towers with feet flat on the floor for stability.
Reach down and grab one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-facing position; start with arms out to the sides at about shoulder height and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (squeeze the shoulder blades together and down) to stabilize the shoulders before beginning each rep.

Exhale and bring both hands up and together in a wide arc over your chest, maintaining the slight elbow bend and focusing on squeezing the pectorals at the top; hands should meet or nearly meet above the sternum.

Inhale and slowly lower the handles back out to the starting position in a controlled manner until you feel a gentle stretch across the chest — do not let the elbows drop excessively behind the line of the shoulders.
Keep the torso stable and avoid arching the lower back; maintain control of the cables throughout the entire set and adjust weight if you cannot control the return.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then carefully release the handles and sit up before changing weight or leaving the bench.
exercises.description: A supine cable fly performed on a flat bench using low-set pulleys to isolate the chest by bringing the hands together in a wide arc. The movement emphasizes horizontal shoulder adduction while keeping a slight bend in the elbows to protect the joints.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Pec Fly
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine handles so they are roughly at mid-chest/shoulder height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the lever machine handles so they are roughly at mid-chest/shoulder height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the lever arms with feet about shoulder-width apart and a soft bend in the knees; maintain a neutral spine and a slight forward lean from the hips.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-in grip and raise your arms out to the sides so they are roughly parallel to the floor; maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout (start position).
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest by squeezing the pectorals, keeping the elbow angle constant and avoiding shoulder shrugging or excessive forward elbow travel.
Pause briefly at peak contraction with hands meeting or close in front of the chest, focusing on contracting the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly control the levers back to the start position until you feel a stretch across the chest; do not let the weights slam back.
Repeat for the prescribed reps. Keep shoulders down and back, core braced, and avoid rotating the torso or using momentum.
exercises.description: A standing machine lever pec fly that isolates the chest by bringing two arms together in front of the body from a wide, horizontal start. Performed standing with a slight forward lean to maximize pectoral contraction while the delts and serratus anterior assist and stabilize.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Reverse Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a reverse (supinated) grip so your palms face toward your head; retract your shoulder blades and keep your chest lifted.
Start with the dumbbells at chest level, elbows tucked roughly 30–45 degrees from your torso and forearms vertical.
Inhale, then press the dumbbells upward and slightly back in a controlled path until your arms are extended but not aggressively hyperextended at the elbows.
Exhale as you reach the top, maintain wrist alignment so the weight sits over the forearms, and keep the shoulders down and back.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting chest position under control, feeling the stretch in the pectorals while keeping the reverse grip and elbow position.
Repeat for the desired reps. Use a spotter for heavier loads and avoid excessive wrist extension or flaring of the elbows.
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell press performed with a reverse (supinated) grip that emphasizes the upper/inner pectorals and increases biceps and forearm involvement compared with a standard press.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Fly
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered (split) stance or feet shoulder-width apart with a slight forward lean from the hips so you have stable balance; brace your core.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip and take one step forward until you feel light tension in the cables; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout.
Start with your arms out to the sides roughly at shoulder height (or slightly higher for a high-cable fly) and chest lifted; retract your shoulder blades slightly to protect the joints.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a controlled arc in front of your chest at midline, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the finish.
Pause briefly with hands meeting or slightly overlapping, then inhale and slowly resist the cables as you return your arms to the start position using the same arcing path.
Keep the movement smooth and avoid locking the elbows, excessive torso rotation, or shrugging the shoulders; maintain core tension for stability.
exercises.description: Standing cable fly performed with the pulleys set high to mid-high engages the chest by bringing the arms together in a wide arc; the shoulders and core stabilize the movement. It emphasizes the pectoral fibers with controlled concentric adduction and slow eccentric return.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench with knees secured under the pads and feet flat on the floor (seated position).
Reach up and grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Sit tall with a neutral spine and chest slightly lifted; lean back only a few degrees (small recline) — do not arch the lower back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down and back, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/clavicle while leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Hold and squeeze the lats briefly at the bottom of the movement without leaning excessively backward.
Slowly and under control allow the bar to return to the start position until the arms are nearly fully extended and the shoulder blades re-expand.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining controlled tempo and avoiding momentum or swinging.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar targets the latissimus dorsi with a wide overhand grip, pulling the bar down toward the upper chest. The movement also engages the biceps, rear delts and upper back muscles to stabilize and complete the pull.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Lean forward from the hips and reach to grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders rounded forward (scapulae protracted).
instructions: Sit on the bench/seat facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent; select an appropriate weight.
Lean forward from the hips and reach to grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders rounded forward (scapulae protracted).
Keep a neutral spine and a slight chest-up posture; initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades (pull shoulders down and back) before bending the elbows.

Pull the handle toward your torso (near the lower chest/upper abdomen) driving the elbows back and keeping them close to your sides; squeeze the shoulder blades together at the end of the concentric phase.
Pause briefly at full contraction, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding excessive torso swing or leaning back.
Slowly extend the arms and allow the shoulder blades to protract as you return to the starting position with control, keeping tension on the cable.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining neutral spine, controlled tempo, and full scapular retraction on each rep.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed from a seated position that primarily targets the latissimus dorsi while working the mid/upper back and arms. It develops scapular retraction and elbow flexion strength through a controlled pull and release.

exercises.name: Cable High Row with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.

Position an incline bench or chest pad in front of the machine so you can sit facing the pulleys with your chest pressed firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust pad height so torso is supported and arms can reach the handles overhead.
Sit tall with chest against the pad, neutral neck, core braced and shoulders down away from ears; reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Begin with arms nearly extended overhead, scapulae slightly protracted; inhale to prepare.
Exhale and pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest by driving your elbows down and out, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the end range.
Hold the contracted position briefly (1–2 seconds) while maintaining a supported chest and no torso rotation.
Slowly release the handles under control, allowing the arms to extend back to the start while keeping tension on the cables and scapulae engaged.
Repeat for desired reps; avoid using momentum, leaning away from the pad, or shrugging the shoulders upward.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported cable high row performed from dual high pulleys; you pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest while the chest rests against the pad to isolate the upper back. Emphasizes scapular retraction and posterior shoulder activation with reduced lower-back involvement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown (pro lat bar)
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
instructions: Sit on the pulldown bench with knees secured under the thigh pads and feet flat on the floor (seated, knees braced).
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Begin tall with a neutral spine and a slight lean back from the hips (not from the lower back), chest lifted and eyes forward.
Inhale, then exhale and initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to engage the lats.
Pull the bar down in a controlled arc toward the upper chest (sternal area), leading with the elbows and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the bottom and squeeze the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with straight arms, fully extending the shoulders while maintaining a stable torso.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum; keep movement controlled and driven by the lats.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar performed from a tall seated position, pulling the bar down to the upper chest to target the latissimus dorsi and upper back. Focus is on scapular retraction and controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Landmine Bent over Row with V-bar (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Set up: anchor one end of the barbell in the landmine pivot and load plates on the free end; attach a V-handle to the bar near the plates.
instructions: Set up: anchor one end of the barbell in the landmine pivot and load plates on the free end; attach a V-handle to the bar near the plates.
Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell between your legs; slightly bend the knees and hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from horizontal (bent-over position).
Keep a neutral spine and engaged core; shoulders should be slightly protracted with arms extended to start and palms facing each other on the V-handle (neutral grip).
Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and driving the elbows back and up, pulling the V-handle toward your lower ribs/upper abdomen while keeping the torso stable.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the top for a brief pause, ensuring elbows travel close to the body and posterior delts/traps assist the movement.
Lower the bar under control by extending the arms until near full extension while maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and tension in the core and posterior chain.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale as you return to the start; avoid rounding the back, excessive torso swing, or using momentum from the hips.
exercises.description: A two-handed neutral-grip row performed with a V-handle on a landmine/barbell pivot, using a hip hinge to pull the bar toward the lower rib/upper abdomen to emphasize the lats and upper back while maintaining a strong torso position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it securely on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it securely on the floor.
Select a pair of dumbbells and sit at the end of the bench, then carefully lie prone with your chest supported on the bench and feet on the floor or toes braced for stability.
Let your arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a neutral or palms-facing-in grip on the dumbbells; maintain a neutral neck and spine.
Engage your core and pull your shoulder blades back and down to initiate the movement.
Drive your elbows up and back, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs/waist while keeping elbows close to the body and squeezing the upper back at the top.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular retraction and avoid shrugging the shoulders toward the ears.
Lower the dumbbells slowly under control to the starting position until the arms are nearly extended, keeping tension in the back throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the concentric (pull) and breathing in on the eccentric (lower).
Keep movements controlled, avoid torso rotation or using momentum, and reduce weight if you cannot maintain scapular control.
exercises.description: A prone row performed on an incline bench where the chest is supported and dumbbells are pulled toward the torso to emphasize the upper back. It uses a controlled scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the mid/upper back and pulling musculature.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the wide bar with a pronated (overhand) grip wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Stand facing a low cable pulley with feet about hip-width apart and attach a straight bar or handle to the low setting.
instructions: Stand facing a low cable pulley with feet about hip-width apart and attach a straight bar or handle to the low setting.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angle), keeping a slight bend in the knees; this is a bent-over position.
Maintain a neutral spine and head in line with the torso, bracing your core and keeping the chest lifted throughout the set.
Reach forward and grab the bar with a comfortable grip (overhand or neutral), allowing the cable to pull your arms fully extended.
Initiate the row by driving your elbows straight back toward your hips, squeezing the shoulder blades together and leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Pause briefly at full contraction, keeping the torso stable and avoiding any excessive torso rotation or momentum.
Slowly extend your arms under control back to the start position until the lats are stretched and the shoulder blades open, inhale on the return.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements controlled and spine neutral; reduce weight if you feel lower back rounding or excessive swinging.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with a low cable pulley that targets the upper back by retracting the shoulder blades while the torso is hinged forward. The movement also engages the lats, arms and posterior shoulder while the posterior chain and core stabilize the body.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet about hip-width apart and knees unlocked (soft bend).
instructions: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet about hip-width apart and knees unlocked (soft bend).
Grip a barbell with both hands, arms fully extended, using an overhand (pronated) or mixed grip; hold the bar at thigh level in front of your body.
Keep your torso tall, chest up and head neutral — avoid leaning or using lower back to lift.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears by contracting the traps and levator scapulae; do not rotate or roll the shoulders.
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top to maximize contraction while keeping arms straight and elbows relaxed.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start, controlling the descent through the full range.
Repeat for the desired reps; maintain a steady tempo, avoid using momentum, and keep a firm grip (forearms engaged) throughout.
exercises.description: Standing barbell shrugs elevate the shoulders to target the upper trapezius and surrounding scapular elevators; the movement is a vertical scapular elevation with the arms kept straight. Perform controlled shrugs up toward the ears, pause at the top, then lower slowly to the start.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
instructions: Sit on the machine seat with knees bent and feet planted on the footrests; brace your chest against the chest pad (if present) and sit upright.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
Set your spine neutral, retract your shoulder blades slightly, and maintain a tall chest—do not round your back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back, pulling the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and feel the lats and mid-back engage; avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start position in a controlled manner until your arms are nearly straight and the lats are stretched.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping consistent tempo, full range of motion, and a firm grip throughout.
exercises.description: Seated pronated-grip lever row performed on a plate-loaded machine to develop the lats and mid-upper back by pulling a lever toward the torso using an overhand grip. Emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining an upright, braced seated position.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Single Arm Wide Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and thigh pads so your feet are flat and your thighs are secured under the pads while seated upright.
instructions: Adjust the seat and thigh pads so your feet are flat and your thighs are secured under the pads while seated upright.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the single wide overhead handle with one hand using a pronated (overhand) grip, arm fully extended overhead.
Brace your core and keep your chest up; allow a slight backward lean at the hips but avoid excessive torso swing.
Initiate the movement by driving the elbow down and back, pulling the handle toward the side of your chest/outer ribcage while leading with the elbow and squeezing the lat and scapula.
Pause briefly at the bottom with the lat contracted and shoulder blade depressed, avoiding shrugging the shoulder toward the ear.
Slowly and under control extend the arm back to the start position until the elbow is nearly straight, keeping tension on the lat throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch arms; maintain consistent form and avoid using momentum or rotating the torso to lift the weight.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm wide lat pulldown performed on a lever station to isolate each lat while using a wide overhead handle. The movement pulls the handle down and back, emphasizing elbow-driven scapular depression and lat contraction.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench with knees secured under the pads and feet flat on the floor (seated position).
Reach up and grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Sit tall with a neutral spine and chest slightly lifted; lean back only a few degrees (small recline) — do not arch the lower back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down and back, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/clavicle while leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Hold and squeeze the lats briefly at the bottom of the movement without leaning excessively backward.
Slowly and under control allow the bar to return to the start position until the arms are nearly fully extended and the shoulder blades re-expand.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining controlled tempo and avoiding momentum or swinging.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar targets the latissimus dorsi with a wide overhand grip, pulling the bar down toward the upper chest. The movement also engages the biceps, rear delts and upper back muscles to stabilize and complete the pull.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.description: Shoulder Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward (or slightly rotated) and elbows bent roughly 90 degrees, wrists stacked over elbows.
Inhale, then exhale as you press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly extended overhead; avoid fully locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the shoulders down and chest lifted (do not shrug excessively).
Inhale as you slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Keep the back pressed against the bench and avoid excessive lower-back arching or leaning; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press performed from a back-supported bench to drive the weights overhead, primarily working the shoulder (deltoid) muscles with triceps and upper traps assisting. The movement emphasizes vertical pressing strength and shoulder stability.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Use a lighter weight or a spotter if form breaks down or if pressing heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press is a compound pressing movement performed from a seated, back‑supported position that primarily develops the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Use a lighter weight or a spotter if form breaks down or if pressing heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press is a compound pressing movement performed from a seated, back‑supported position that primarily develops the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Single Arm Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handle starts at about shoulder level when seated and select an appropriate weight on the lever stack.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handle starts at about shoulder level when seated and select an appropriate weight on the lever stack.
Sit upright with your back firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core to prevent excessive spinal extension.
Grip the handle with one hand (neutral or overhand grip) so your elbow is roughly under or slightly in front of the shoulder; rest your free hand on your abdomen or thigh for balance.
Exhale and press the handle upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arm is nearly straight (avoid locking the elbow or shrugging the shoulder).
Pause briefly at the top with shoulder blades stable, then inhale and slowly lower the handle back down to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired reps with one arm, then switch sides; maintain a steady tempo, keep the wrist neutral, and avoid leaning or twisting the torso.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm shoulder press performed on a lever machine: press the machine handle upward from shoulder height using one arm to work the delts while the opposite arm can rest for stability. Controlled concentric press and eccentric descent emphasize shoulder strength and stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing the body) and arms resting by your sides.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing the body) and arms resting by your sides.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows and maintain a neutral spine and tucked chin throughout the movement.
Exhale and raise both dumbbells straight forward from the hips to about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor), leading with the elbows and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping shoulders down (avoid shrugging) and avoid using body swing or back extension to lift the weight.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form; if form breaks, reduce the weight or perform single-arm raises to maintain control.
exercises.description: A standing resistance exercise where you raise dumbbells straight in front of you to shoulder height to emphasize the anterior delts. It uses a controlled motion with core bracing to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Cable Front Raise
exercises.notes: Step 1: Attach a straight bar or single handle to the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Step 1: Attach a straight bar or single handle to the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Step 2: Stand facing the cable machine with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing upright).
Step 3: Grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms down) about shoulder-width, arms extended down in front with a slight bend at the elbows.
Step 4: Maintain a neutral spine and keep shoulders back and down; avoid leaning or using momentum.
Step 5: Exhale and raise the bar straight forward and up with the arms (leading with the elbows) until your hands reach about shoulder height.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the top while keeping tension on the cable and the core engaged.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting position under control, keeping the slight elbow bend throughout.
Step 8: Repeat for the desired reps; avoid swinging the torso or using excessive weight to maintain strict delt activation.
exercises.description: A standing cable front raise performed from a low pulley that lifts the arms straight in front of the body to shoulder height, primarily working the front portion of the shoulder. It uses a controlled movement with a slight elbow bend to isolate the anterior delts.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Barbell Close Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench in a rack or on a bench press station and load the barbell safely; position the bar at arm level for easy unracking.
instructions: Set up a flat bench in a rack or on a bench press station and load the barbell safely; position the bar at arm level for easy unracking.
Lie supine (on your back) on the bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, shoulders retracted and pressed into the bench for stability.
Grip the barbell with a close grip: hands about shoulder-width apart (index fingers roughly in line with the shoulders) or slightly narrower; keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your sternum; keep a tight core and stable foot drive.
Inhale and lower the bar under control to the lower chest / sternum area, keeping elbows tucked closer to your body (about 45 degrees or less from the torso) to emphasize the triceps.
Pause briefly with the bar lightly touching the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward by extending the elbows until arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended.
Maintain wrist alignment and shoulder position throughout; avoid flaring the elbows wide and keep the movement vertical over the chest.
Rerack the bar with control after the set; use a spotter when lifting heavy or if you are near failure.
Progress by increasing load gradually, or modify grip width slightly to change emphasis; stop or reduce range if you feel joint pain (especially in wrists or elbows).
exercises.description: A compound supine pressing exercise performed with a narrow (close) grip to place primary emphasis on the triceps while still recruiting the pectorals and anterior delts. Performed on a flat bench, the bar is lowered to the chest and pressed back to full elbow extension.

exercises.name: Cable Triceps Pushdown (V bar)
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to the high position and attach a V‑bar.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to the high position and attach a V‑bar. Select an appropriate weight that allows controlled reps.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip‑width apart and a slight stagger if preferred for balance; grasp the V‑bar with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip.
Start with your elbows bent ~90°, upper arms close to your sides, and a slight forward lean at the hips so your torso is stable and shoulders are over your hips.
Brace your core and keep your upper arms stationary; exhale and press the V‑bar down by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight but not locked aggressively.
Squeeze the triceps at the bottom of the movement for a brief pause (1 second) while maintaining neutral wrists.
Inhale and slowly return the V‑bar to the starting position under control, allowing the elbows to bend until the forearms are roughly parallel to the floor or reach the chosen start depth.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding use of momentum.
Common form cues: keep elbows tucked to your sides (do not let them drift forward or flare out), maintain neutral wrists, and avoid leaning back or swinging the body to complete reps.
exercises.description: Standing cable triceps pushdown using a V‑bar targets the triceps through elbow extension while the V‑grip emphasizes the medial head and allows a neutral wrist position. Performed from a high pulley, the movement is a strict elbow hinge with minimal shoulder movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Biceps Curl
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing forward (supinated grip) and arms fully extended.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and fixed in position throughout the movement.
Exhale and curl the dumbbells by bending your elbows until the weights reach shoulder level, squeezing the biceps at the top.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining upright posture and avoid leaning back or using momentum.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with full elbow extension under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on strict form and minimizing shoulder or torso movement.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell biceps curl isolates the biceps by flexing the elbow against resistance while standing upright; perform controlled reps without swinging to maximize tension on the biceps.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
